options.text: Acknowledge frustration, inform them this is resolvable now, and offer to complete it or escalate
options.explain: Correct. Honor the feeling, give them the fast resolution path in writing, and preserve their choice. That's the customer-respect move that still leverages the agent's capability.
options.condensedText: Sentiment-thresholded escalation.

options.text: Call `escalate_to_human` immediately to honor the customer's request
options.explain: Unnecessary queueing when the issue is one tool call away. Frustrates the customer further by adding waiting to an already-simple case.
options.condensedText: Rules engine on segments / categories.

options.text: Process the refund via `process_refund` to resolve the underlying issue, then inform them it's complete
options.explain: Takes unilateral action after the customer explicitly asked to speak to someone â overrides their stated preference.
options.condensedText: Escalate after three failed tool calls.

options.text: Ask what specifically hasn't worked in previous attempts before deciding whether to escalate or resolve automatically
options.explain: Interrogating a frustrated customer about past failures is the opposite of what they asked for.
options.condensedText: Instruct the agent to escalate when the customer requests a human, when the issue requires policy exceptions, or when the agent cannot make meaningful progress.

questions.pattern: Escalation decisions are model-driven judgements over intent and capability. Reserve thresholds for safety nets.

questions.text:
The agent verifies customer identity through a multi-step process before resetting passwords. During testing, you notice that after the customer answers the third verification question, the agent asks them to provide their name again, as if the earlier exchange never happened.
What's the most likely cause of this behavior?
questions.condensedText: Frustrated customer demands a real person "NOW". No tools called yet. What should the agent do?

options.text: The verification tool is clearing the agent's internal state after each successful validation step.
options.explain: Tools don't clear agent state â conversation state lives in the messages array you send.
options.condensedText: Try to resolve first; escalate only if the customer repeats.

options.text: The prompt lacks instructions telling Claude to remember information across multiple exchanges.
options.explain: Memory across turns isn't achieved by an instruction â it comes from passing the prior turns into the next request.
options.condensedText: Escalate immediately with transcript.

options.text: The conversation history isn't being passed in subsequent API requests.
options.explain: Correct. The API is stateless. Each request must include the full messages array. If you only send the latest turn, the model has no memory of earlier ones â exactly the 'ask for the name again' symptom.
options.condensedText: First call get_customer and lookup_order to gather account context, then escalate to a human.

options.text: Claude's memory retention is limited to two conversational turns by default, requiring explicit configuration to extend it.
options.explain: No such default limit exists. Context is bounded by the window, and history length is under your control.
options.condensedText: Ask a targeted question first.

questions.pattern: Honour the escalation request, but front-load context-gathering so the human starts informed.

questions.text:
Production logs reveal inconsistent error handling: when `lookup_order` fails, the agent sometimes retries 5+ times (wasteful when the order ID doesn't exist), sometimes escalates immediately (premature for temporary network issues), and sometimes asks users for clarification (inappropriate when the issue is a backend permission error). Investigation shows your MCP tool returns uniform error responses: {"isError": true, "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Operation failed"}]}. The agent cannot distinguish between error types.
What's the most effective improvement?
questions.condensedText: Uniform plain-text errors lead to inconsistent agent behaviour (over-retrying, premature escalation, asking users about backend issues). Best improvement?

options.text: Enhance error responses with structured metadata: include errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetryable boolean, and a description of what caused the failure.
options.explain: Correct. Give the agent the information it needs to make the right decision: category, retryability, and a human-readable cause. That replaces guessing with deterministic policy.
options.condensedText: Add an analyze_error tool after failures.

options.text: Create an `analyze_error` MCP tool the agent calls after any failure to determine the error category and recommended action.
options.explain: Adds an extra round-trip for something the original tool already knows. Put the metadata in the original response.
options.condensedText: Few-shot examples for error-text parsing.

options.text: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff in your MCP server for all errors, returning to the agent only after retries are exhausted.
options.explain: Blanket retries hurt on permanent errors (order not found) and hide useful distinctions from the agent.
options.condensedText: Exponential backoff for all errors.

options.text: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to interpret error message patterns and select appropriate responses for each.
options.explain: If the tool returns 'Operation failed' for every failure, no amount of few-shots can extract category info that isn't there.
options.condensedText: Enhance error responses with structured metadata: errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetriable, and a description of the cause.

questions.pattern: Error responses are typed data. Category, retriability, cause — all structured, all explicit.

questions.text: When the agent calls `lookup_order` and receives order details showing the item was purchased 45 days ago, how does the agentic loop determine whether to call `process_refund` or `escalate_to_human` next?
questions.condensedText: Three issues across a session approaching context limits. At turn 48 the customer asks about issue 1. Best strategy?

options.text: The orchestration layer automatically routes to the next tool based on the order's status field.
options.explain: There's no implicit orchestration layer picking tools from a field. The model drives tool selection.
options.condensedText: Sliding window of last 30 turns.

options.text: The agent follows a pre-configured decision tree mapping order attributes to specific tool calls.
options.explain: Agentic loops are model-driven, not decision-tree-driven. Hardcoded trees are the opposite of what the agent pattern is for.
options.condensedText: Rely on tools to re-fetch.

options.text: The order details are added to the conversation and the model reasons about which action to take.
options.explain: Correct. The agentic loop works by appending `tool_result` messages to the conversation and letting the model decide the next step on each turn. That's how 45 days â refund vs. escalate gets resolved.
options.condensedText: Summarise earlier turns as prose.

options.text: The agent executes the remaining steps in a tool sequence planned at the start of the request.
options.explain: There's no upfront, committed plan â the agent chooses each next step based on the latest context.
options.condensedText: Extract and persist structured issue data (order IDs, amounts, statuses) into a separate context layer.

questions.pattern: Multi-issue conversations need per-issue structured state, not single-stream summary.

questions.text:
A customer sends: "This is frustrating. I've explained my issue twice and nothing is being resolved. I want to talk to a real person NOW." The agent has not yet called any tools to investigate their account.
What should the agent do?
questions.condensedText: lookup_order returns 40+ fields; tool outputs dominate context. Customer mentions two more orders. Best fix before more calls?

options.text: Acknowledge the frustration and ask one targeted question to understand the specific issue before escalating.
options.explain: Correct. The customer has said 'twice' but you have no context yet. One acknowledging, focused question gives you a shot at first-contact resolution without dismissing the frustration or delaying a potential handoff.
options.condensedText: Proceed without modifying.

options.text: Briefly explain what the agent can help with and offer to resolve the issue quickly, escalating only if the customer repeats their request.
options.explain: Launching into capability-listing at a frustrated customer who asked for a human reads as dismissive.
options.condensedText: Move tool responses to vector DB.

options.text: Immediately call `escalate_to_human` with the conversation history.
options.explain: Escalating with zero tool context creates a cold handoff where the human also starts from scratch.
options.condensedText: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each existing order response, removing verbose details.

options.text: First call `get_customer` and `lookup_order` to gather account context, then escalate to a human agent.
options.explain: Investigating without asking adds latency and doesn't respect the customer's request. A single acknowledging question is faster and more respectful.
options.condensedText: Model summarises each as prose.

questions.pattern: When tool outputs dominate context, projection (field selection) beats summary (paraphrase).

questions.text:
Your agent is handling a billing dispute. After calling `get_customer` and `lookup_order`, it identifies that the dispute involves a promotional pricing error requiring manager approvalâbeyond the agent's authorization level.
How should the workflow handle this mid-process escalation?
questions.condensedText: 25+ turn billing investigation. Refund $847 exceeds $500 authorisation. Human will not see transcript. What context to pass?

options.text: Call `escalate_to_human` passing only the customer's original message.
options.explain: Drops the tool-derived context the agent just gathered, forcing the human to re-do the investigation.
options.condensedText: Verbatim complaint + tool excerpts.

options.text: Compile a structured handoff with customer details, order info, and the identified issue before calling `escalate_to_human`.
options.explain: Correct. A structured brief (who, what order, what issue, why it exceeds auth) lets the human agent pick up instantly. That's the mid-process escalation pattern.
options.condensedText: Diagnosis + amount only.

options.text: Attempt the refund with `process_refund` anyway, escalating only if the system rejects the transaction.
options.explain: Knowingly exceeding authorization is a policy violation â not something to try and hope the system catches.
options.condensedText: A structured summary: customer ID, root cause, refund amount, and recommended action.

options.text: Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call `escalate_to_human` with a reference ID.
options.explain: Adds infrastructure and an extra lookup step when an inline structured brief is simpler and faster.
options.condensedText: Complete transcript with all tool results.

questions.pattern: Handoff = customer ID + root cause + amount/figure + recommended action. Nothing less, nothing more.

questions.text:
A customer raises three separate issues during one session: a refund inquiry (turns 1-15), a subscription question (turns 16-30), and a payment method update (turns 31-45). At turn 48, the customer asks "What happened with my refund?" The conversation is approaching context limits.
What strategy best maintains the agent's ability to address all issues throughout the session?
questions.condensedText: Multi-step identity verification: agent forgets the customer's name after the third question. Most likely cause?

options.text: Extract and persist structured issue data (order IDs, amounts, statuses) into a separate context layer.
options.explain: A sidecar context layer adds engineering and doesn't naturally preserve the conversational narrative the customer expects.
options.condensedText: The conversation history is not being passed in subsequent API requests.

options.text: Rely on MCP tools to re-fetch relevant information on demand when the customer references earlier issues.
options.explain: Re-fetching is fine for freshness, but it doesn't solve the context-length problem â and you lose what was said between the customer and the agent.
options.condensedText: Two-turn memory retention default.

options.text: Summarize earlier turns into a narrative description, preserving full message history only for the active issue.
options.explain: Correct. Progressive summarization compresses stable resolved topics while keeping the active thread verbatim â the classic pattern for long multi-issue conversations near the context limit.
options.condensedText: Tool clears internal state.

options.text: Implement sliding window context that retains the most recent 30 turns.
options.explain: A pure sliding window silently drops the refund issue (turns 1â15) from context, which is exactly what the customer is asking about.
options.condensedText: Prompt lacks memory instructions.

questions.pattern: “The agent forgets” ⇒ first check that the full message history is being sent every call.

questions.text:
When implementing your `lookup_order` MCP tool, the backend sometimes returns errors (e.g., "Order not found" or temporary database failures).
What is the correct pattern for communicating these errors back to the agent?
questions.condensedText: Refunds over $500 must always escalate. Prompts achieve 97% compliance, leaving a 3% failure rate. Required: guaranteed compliance.

options.text: Log the error server-side and return an empty result to avoid confusing the model
options.explain: Returning empty successes makes the agent think no data exists, rather than that something went wrong â a different and worse confusion.
options.condensedText: Tool returns "please escalate".

options.text: Return the error message in the tool result content with the isError flag set to true
options.explain: Correct. MCP's designed pattern: put the error text in the content field and mark isError=true. Claude sees both the failure flag and a readable message to reason about.
options.condensedText: Implement a hook to intercept tool calls; when the refund amount exceeds $500, block the call and invoke human escalation.

options.text: Throw an exception from the tool handler so the agent framework can catch and log it
options.explain: Uncaught exceptions break the tool protocol and don't give the model anything to reason with.
options.condensedText: Stronger system prompt.

options.text: Return a success response with a "status" field indicating the error type
options.explain: Ad-hoc 'status' fields vary across tools and the model has no standard way to interpret them. isError is the standard.
options.condensedText: Few-shot examples.

questions.pattern: Money / compliance / security → hooks. Style / preferences → prompts. Never confuse these.

questions.text:
Your `process_refund` tool returns two types of errors: technical errors ("503 Service Unavailable", "Connection timeout") that are transient (5% of calls), and business errors ("Order exceeds 30-day return window", "Item already refunded") that are permanent (12% of calls). Monitoring shows the agent wastes 3-4 turns retrying business errors that can never succeed. Currently, both error types return only a plain text message to Claude.
What's the most effective way to reduce wasted retries while improving customer-facing response quality?
questions.condensedText: get_customer and lookup_order succeed; process_refund times out. Best balance of firstcontact resolution and error handling?

options.text: Return structured error responses with retryable: false for business errors and a customer-friendly explanation for Claude to use.
options.explain: Correct. A retryable flag tells Claude deterministically 'don't retry,' and a ready-made customer-friendly message improves the outgoing reply. Fixes both problems at once.
options.condensedText: Explain the billing, confirm refund eligibility, acknowledge the system issue, and offer escalation or retry later.

options.text: Add few-shot examples showing how to distinguish retryable from non-retryable errors by parsing error message text.
options.explain: Relying on the model to text-parse error strings is fragile and exactly the instability you see today.
options.condensedText: Escalate immediately.

options.text: Add a `check_refund_eligibility` tool that must be called before `process_refund` to prevent business rule violations.
options.explain: Useful in principle but adds a round-trip to every refund to guard against 12% of cases, and doesn't help when `process_refund` still fails for other business reasons.
options.condensedText: Confirm refund and close.

options.text: Implement automatic retry logic at the tool level for technical errors only, passing business errors to Claude without retries.
options.explain: Hiding transient retries inside the tool can mask latency and takes the model out of the loop on recovery decisions. Business errors also still arrive as a plain string, so the customer-facing response doesn't improve.
options.condensedText: Indefinite retries with backoff.

questions.pattern: Partial success = transparent state report + clear options. Do not claim completion you have not achieved.

questions.text:
Your agent has called `lookup_order` multiple times while investigating a customer's return requests. Each response includes 40+ fields (items, shipping details, payment info, status history). Tool outputs now represent the majority of the conversation's context. The customer mentions two more orders they want to discuss.
What's the most effective approach before making additional lookups?
questions.condensedText: User explicitly requests a human. The agent can resolve in one step. Best response?

options.text: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each existing order response, removing verbose details
options.explain: Correct. Keep the fields that matter for the task and drop the rest. This directly addresses the context-bloat problem before you add two more lookups.
options.condensedText: Process the refund and inform after the fact.

options.text: Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order's key details, replacing structured responses with prose descriptions
options.explain: Prose summaries lose precision for fields the model may need later (exact dates, amounts). Structured pruning is better than paraphrasing.
options.condensedText: Ask what went wrong before.

options.text: Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing, retrieving relevant portions as the conversation continues
options.explain: A vector DB is heavy infrastructure for what is essentially a pruning problem.
options.condensedText: Escalate immediately.

options.text: Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output context
options.explain: Does nothing about the bloat â you're heading for context exhaustion.
options.condensedText: Acknowledge frustration, inform them this is resolvable now, and offer to complete it or escalate.

questions.pattern: When user agency and agent capability point in different directions, offer both options transparently.

questions.text:
Your extraction system processes two document types: standard monthly reports (archived after processing) and urgent exception reports (must trigger business alerts within 30 minutes of receipt). Both use the same JSON schema. You want to minimize API costs while meeting latency requirements.
How should you architect the processing pipeline?
questions.condensedText: Menus use inconsistent formats ($12 vs 12.00, icons vs text). Best reliable approach?

options.text: Submit all documents to the real-time Messages API to ensure consistent processing latency across document types.
options.explain: Consistent but expensive. Standard monthly reports don't need real-time latency and shouldn't pay for it.
options.condensedText: Separate calls per field.

options.text: Submit all documents to the `Batch API` with `custom_ids` for tracking. When results arrive, immediately process urgent documents and trigger delayed alerts for exceptions.
options.explain: Batch has up to a 24-hour window â 30-minute alerting SLAs on exception reports can't be met by routing them through batch.
options.condensedText: Define a strict output schema and include format normalisation rules in your prompt.

options.text: Queue all documents and submit hourly batches, flagging urgent documents for expedited handling when batch results return.
options.explain: Hourly batches still ride the batch SLO and don't guarantee the 30-minute alert window for exceptions.
options.condensedText: Vote across multiple attempts.

options.text: Route standard reports to the `Batch API` for 50% cost savings, and route urgent exception reports to the real-time Messages API.
options.explain: Correct. Match latency profile to document urgency: batch for the bulk (cheap), real-time for the latency-sensitive exceptions (fast). Minimizes cost while meeting SLA.
options.condensedText: Post-process in code.

questions.pattern: Schema + normalisation rules in the prompt = deterministic, single-pass extraction.

questions.text:
Your schema includes a skills: string[] field. Production monitoring reveals three consistency issues: (1) compound phrases like "Python and SQL" are sometimes kept as one entry, sometimes split; (2) implied but unstated skills occasionally appear in extractions; (3) similar documents produce wildly different array lengths (5-10 vs 40+ entries). Your prompt currently says "Extract all skills mentioned."
What's the most effective improvement?
questions.condensedText: Property-type enum fails on 8% of listings due to novel categories. New types keep appearing. Best long-term fix?

options.text: Add few-shot examples demonstrating compound phrase handling, explicit mention criteria, and appropriate entry granularity.
options.explain: Correct. All three issues are about the model's interpretation of what counts as 'a skill.' Few-shot examples teach the pattern concretely â split vs. not-split, mentioned vs. inferred, appropriate granularity.
options.condensedText: Continuously expand the enum.

options.text: Add constraints: "Extract 10-20 skills maximum, one skill per entry, only explicitly named skills."
options.explain: Hard count caps are arbitrary and can force the model to drop real skills or invent filler to hit the range.
options.condensedText: Free-form string + normalisation.

options.text: Add post-extraction normalization that maps skills to a canonical taxonomy and deduplicates similar entries.
options.explain: Post-processing can't fix inferred-but-not-mentioned skills or choose the right split for 'Python and SQL' â that decision has to be made at extraction time.
options.condensedText: Add an "other" value to the enum with a separate property_type_detail string field for specifics when "other" is selected.

options.text: Enrich the schema to {skill: string, confidence: float, `source_quote`: string}[] to capture extraction metadata.
options.explain: Useful signal, but doesn't address the underlying inconsistency in how skills are parsed in the first place.
options.condensedText: Few-shot examples mapping new types to nearest existing value.

questions.pattern: For “mostly bounded but extensible” categorical fields: enum + “other” + detail field.

questions.text: Your system has been operating with 100% human review for 3 months. Analysis shows that extractions with model confidence >90% have 97% accuracy overall. To reduce reviewer workload, you plan to automate high-confidence extractions. Before deploying, what validation step is most critical?
questions.condensedText: Free-text JSON occasionally fails to parse. Most reliable approach to schema-compliant structured data?

options.text: Analyze accuracy by document type and field to verify high-confidence extractions perform consistently across all segments, not just in aggregate.
options.explain: Correct. Aggregate accuracy hides segment failures â one document type could be 70% while others are 99%. Auto-routing by overall number alone risks systemic errors in the weak segments.
options.condensedText: Define a tool with a JSON schema specifying the expected structure; use tool_use to constrain output to schema-compliant JSON.

options.text: Compare accuracy at different confidence thresholds (85%, 90%, 95%) to find the optimal cutoff that maximizes automation while minimizing errors.
options.explain: Threshold tuning is worth doing, but only after you know the accuracy holds uniformly across segments â otherwise you're optimizing a number that lies.
options.condensedText: Better prompt instructions and examples.

options.text: Run a two-week pilot routing 25% of high-confidence extractions directly to downstream systems and monitor error reports.
options.explain: A pilot is good practice, but it's downstream of the segment analysis â if a segment is systematically wrong, you'll learn it by harming users.
options.condensedText: Retry loop on parse failures.

options.text: Verify that 97% accuracy meets requirements for all downstream systems that consume the extracted data.
options.explain: An important product question, but it assumes the 97% holds everywhere â again, that's what segment analysis verifies first.
options.condensedText: Regex extraction from prose.

questions.pattern: “Structured output” = tool use with a schema. Not prompt + parser.

questions.text:
Your extraction pipeline processes contracts that frequently include amendments. When a contract contains both original terms and later amendments (e.g., original clause specifies "30-day payment terms" while Amendment 1 changes this to "45 days"), the model inconsistently extracts one value or the other with no indication of which applies.
What's the most effective approach to improve extraction accuracy for documents with amendments?
questions.condensedText: All fields nullable, yet model fabricates plausible values for fields not present in source. Best fix?

options.text: Redesign the schema so amended fields capture multiple values, each with source location and effective date.
options.explain: Correct. Amendments are structurally about versioned values. A schema with value + source location + effective date models the domain correctly and stops forcing the model to pick one.
options.condensedText: Second LLM call for verification.

options.text: Add prompt instructions to always extract the most recent amendment value and ignore superseded original terms.
options.explain: Downstream consumers sometimes need the original too (e.g., for dispute resolution). Hard-coding 'most recent wins' throws that away.
options.condensedText: Upgrade model tier.

options.text: Preprocess documents with a classifier that identifies and removes superseded sections before the main extraction step.
options.explain: Deleting sections with a classifier can accidentally strip content that's still legally effective â amendments often modify subsets of clauses.
options.condensedText: Make all fields required.

options.text: Implement post-extraction validation using pattern matching to detect amendments and flag those extractions for manual review.
options.explain: Routes every amended contract to a human â expensive, and still doesn't give downstream systems a structured answer.
options.condensedText: Add prompt instructions to return null for any field where information is not directly stated in the source.

questions.pattern: For absence: schema allows null, prompt requires null. Both are needed.

questions.text:
Your extraction system implements automatic retries when validation fails. On each retry, the specific validation error is appended to the prompt. This retry-with-error-feedback approach resolves most failures within 2-3 attempts.
For which failure pattern would additional retries be LEAST effective?
questions.condensedText: Contracts with amendments produce inconsistent extractions because the schema has one slot per field. Best fix?

options.text: The model extracts keywords as a nested object organized by category when the schema requires a flat array of strings
options.explain: A structural mismatch the model can definitely correct given feedback about the expected shape.
options.condensedText: Preprocess to strip superseded sections.

options.text: The model extracts citation counts as locale-formatted strings ("1,234") when the schema requires integers
options.explain: The required number is present in the document â the model just needs to stop formatting it. Easy for feedback-retry to fix.
options.condensedText: Pattern-matching post-validation flag.

options.text: The model extracts dates as ISO 8601 datetime strings ("2023-03-15T00:00:00Z") when the schema requires only the date portion (YYYY-MM-DD)
options.explain: Pure formatting fix â the underlying value is correct and the model can trim the time portion on retry.
options.condensedText: Redesign the schema so amended fields capture multiple values, each with source location and effective date.

options.text: The model extracts "et al." for co-authors when the full list exists only in an external document not in the input
options.explain: Correct. No amount of retrying teaches the model information that isn't in the input. Retry-with-error-feedback only fixes mistakes the model could have gotten right from the source.
options.condensedText: Prompt: always pick the most recent amendment.

questions.pattern: Schema should mirror the structure of the underlying data, including temporal variants.

questions.text:
Your extraction pipeline processes restaurant menus and must output structured JSON with fields for item names, descriptions, prices, and dietary tags. Some menus use inconsistent formattingâprices as "$12" vs "12.00", dietary info as icons vs text.
What's the most reliable approach?
questions.condensedText: Retry-with-error-feedback resolves most failures in 2�3 attempts. For which failure pattern would the strategy be least likely to benefit from continued retries?

options.text: Use separate extraction calls for each field to ensure consistent handling of each type.
options.explain: Multiple calls per menu multiplies cost and latency and loses cross-field context (e.g., which dietary icons sit next to which item).
options.condensedText: ISO 8601 vs date-only.

options.text: Extract data as-is and normalize formats in post-processing code after Claude returns.
options.explain: Post-processing a raw blob is brittle â deterministic code has to handle every format permutation. Better to let the model normalize at extraction time.
options.condensedText: Nested object vs flat array.

options.text: Request multiple extraction attempts per document and select the most common format.
options.explain: Ensemble voting is expensive and can still pick a wrong-format majority.
options.condensedText: The model extracts "et al." for co-authors when the full list exists only in an external document not in the input.

options.text: Define a strict output schema and include format normalization rules in your prompt.
options.explain: Correct. Strict schema + explicit normalization rules ('prices as decimal with two places', 'dietary as enumerated tags') lets the model both extract and normalize in one pass.
options.condensedText: Locale-formatted integers.

questions.pattern: Retry-with-error-feedback fixes shape; it does not conjure missing source content.

questions.text:
Your system extracts event metadata (date, location, organizer, `attendee_count`) from news articles using a JSON schema with all nullable fields. During evaluation, you observe the model frequently generates plausible but incorrect values for fields not mentioned in the articleâfor example, outputting "500" for `attendee_count` when the source contains no attendance information.
What's the most effective way to reduce these false extractions?
questions.condensedText: Two document types: standard monthly (archived) and urgent (must alert within 30 minutes). Same schema. Minimise cost without breaking SLAs.

options.text: Add a post-processing step using a second LLM call to verify each extracted value exists in the source document.
options.explain: Expensive second pass for a behavior you can correct at the source â tell the model to return null when the field isn't stated.
options.condensedText: All to Batch; trigger delayed alerts.

options.text: Add prompt instructions to return null for any field where information is not directly stated in the source.
options.explain: Correct. The fields are already nullable; the model just needs an explicit instruction to prefer null over a plausible guess. This is the standard fix for schema-aware hallucination.
options.condensedText: Route standard reports to the Batch API for 50% cost savings; route urgent exception reports to the real-time Messages API.

options.text: Make all schema fields required (non-nullable) with strict validation rules to ensure the model only outputs verifiable data.
options.explain: Required fields force the model to invent values when information is missing â the opposite of what you want.
options.condensedText: All to Messages API.

options.text: Upgrade to a more capable model tier with improved instruction-following to reduce hallucination tendencies.
options.explain: Adds cost and still doesn't tell the model your policy â returning null instead of inventing.
options.condensedText: Hourly batches with expedited flagging.

questions.pattern: Match the API to the SLA. Batch where latency is free; real-time where minutes matter.

questions.text:
After implementing tool use with strict schema definitions, JSON syntax errors are eliminated, but 5% of extractions still have valid JSON with empty arrays or null values for required fields like citations and methodology. Spot-checking reveals that source documents contain this information, but in varied formatsâinline citations vs. bibliographies, methodology sections vs. details embedded in introductions.
What's the most effective way to address these failures?
questions.condensedText: Specs vary across sections of the same document; detailed specs table is correct 90% of the time. Best approach?

options.text: Implement retry logic that re-sends requests when validation detects empty required fields.
options.explain: Retry on the same prompt won't suddenly teach the model to recognize methodology embedded in an intro. The extraction capability is the gap.
options.condensedText: Add conflict_detected flag for manual review.

options.text: Build a regex-based post-processing layer that scans source documents for citation patterns and methodology keywords, populating empty fields when the model fails to extract.
options.explain: Regex over prose is brittle, misses the nuanced cases (intro-embedded methodology), and ships silently wrong data.
options.condensedText: Reject on conflict; require document correction.

options.text: Modify your schema to make citations and methodology optional, and flag incomplete records for manual review rather than failing validation.
options.explain: Papers over the symptom. The fields really are required downstream â routing them to humans trades one problem for reviewer workload.
options.condensedText: Include extraction instructions specifying preference for values from the detailed specs table when multiple values exist, keeping the single-value schema.

options.text: Add few-shot examples demonstrating extractions from documents with varied structuresâshowing how to identify citations in different formats and locate methodology details across section types.
options.explain: Correct. The failure mode is the model not recognizing varied formats. Concrete examples across the format distribution directly raise recall on the 5%.
options.condensedText: Array of all values + source locations.

questions.pattern: Domain knowledge (“the spec table is authoritative”) belongs in the prompt, not the data model.

questions.text:
Your extraction pipeline processes invoices and extracts line items, subtotals, tax amounts, and grand totals. During evaluation, you discover that in 18% of extractions, the sum of extracted line item amounts doesn't match the extracted grand totalâsometimes due to OCR errors in the source document, sometimes due to extraction mistakes by the model. Downstream accounting systems reject records with mismatched totals.
What's the most effective approach to improve extraction reliability?
questions.condensedText: Reviewers can handle 5% of volume. Best basis for routing extractions to review?

options.text: Add a "`calculated_total`" field where the model sums extracted line items alongside a "`stated_total`" field. Flag records for human review when values differ.
options.explain: Correct. Capturing both values makes the discrepancy a first-class signal â you catch OCR errors and extraction mistakes uniformly, and you can route only the mismatched 18% to humans.
options.condensedText: Random 5%.

options.text: Extract line items and totals independently, then use a separate validation model to reconcile discrepancies by determining which extracted values are most likely correct.
options.explain: A reconciliation model can mask OCR errors by silently picking one number over another â the accounting system still gets wrong data without a flag.
options.condensedText: Route extractions where the model indicates low confidence or where source documents contain ambiguous or contradictory information.

options.text: Add few-shot examples demonstrating invoices where extracted line items sum correctly to the stated total, encouraging the model to produce mathematically consistent extractions.
options.explain: Can nudge the model toward consistency, but when the source itself is internally inconsistent (OCR errors), the model has to choose one 'wrong'.
options.condensedText: Route by entity type (financial, dates) regardless of confidence.

options.text: Implement post-processing that automatically adjusts line item amounts proportionally when their sum doesn't match the stated total.
options.explain: Silent financial data rewrites are dangerous â you'd be fabricating line items for a downstream accounting system.
options.condensedText: Review only when downstream reports issues.

questions.pattern: Limited human capacity ⇒ uncertainty-driven sampling.

questions.text:
Your pipeline uses a tool called `extract_metadata` with a JSON schema for paper details. You've also defined `lookup_citations` and `verify_doi` tools for enrichment. During testing, you notice that when users include requests like "extract the metadata and tell me how cited it is," Claude sometimes calls `lookup_citations` first, which fails because it needs the DOI that `extract_metadata` would provide.
What's the most effective way to ensure structured metadata extraction happens first?
questions.condensedText: Calendar invites JSON. Downstream rejects malformed JSON. Most reliable approach?

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to "any" so Claude must use a tool, combined with system prompt instructions prioritizing `extract_metadata`.
options.explain: 'any' forces a tool call but doesn't force the right one. You'd still see `lookup_citations` picked first.
options.condensedText: Output-only instructions + retry on failure.

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to "auto" and reorder the tool definitions so `extract_metadata` appears first in the tools array, since Claude prioritizes earlier-listed tools.
options.explain: There's no documented ordering preference to rely on; this assumes behavior that isn't contractual.
options.condensedText: Detailed JSON instructions in prompt.

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to {"type": "tool", "name": "`extract_metadata`"} and process the enrichment requests in subsequent turns after receiving the extracted metadata.
options.explain: Correct. `tool_choice`=specific-tool deterministically forces `extract_metadata` on the first turn. Then you hand control back to 'auto' to let the model use citations/DOI enrichment with the metadata in context.
options.condensedText: Pre-fill opening brace.

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to {"type": "tool", "name": "`extract_metadata`"} for every API call in the pipeline, ensuring Claude always extracts metadata before any enrichment can occur.
options.explain: Pinning `extract_metadata` on every call prevents the enrichment tools from ever being used.
options.condensedText: Define a tool with the target schema as input parameters and have Claude call it with the extracted data.

questions.pattern: Tool use = schema-enforced output. Prompts = best-effort.

questions.text:
Your extraction uses tool use with a JSON schema where `property_type` is defined as an enum: ['house', 'apartment', 'condo', 'townhouse']. After deployment, 8% of extractions fail schema validation. Investigation reveals listings mention many uncommon property typesâ"studio", "loft", "duplex", "mobile home", "tiny house", "converted warehouse"âand new types continue appearing regularly.
What's the most effective long-term solution?
questions.condensedText: 97% aggregate accuracy at high confidence. Planning to auto-process high-confidence cases. Most critical validation before deployment?

options.text: Continuously expand the enum to include newly observed property types and add monitoring for additional edge cases.
options.explain: Whack-a-mole enum maintenance â every new listing style risks another validation failure.
options.condensedText: Analyse accuracy by document type and field to verify high-confidence extractions perform consistently across all segments, not just in aggregate.

options.text: Add an "other" value to your enum with a separate `property_type_detail` string field for specifics when "other" is selected.
options.explain: Correct. Keeps the strong enum for the common cases (clean downstream joins) while giving a well-typed escape hatch that preserves detail. Stable long-term.
options.condensedText: Compare 85% / 90% / 95% thresholds.

options.text: Change `property_type` from an enum to a free-form string and implement a normalization step in post-processing.
options.explain: Loses the validation guarantee entirely and pushes the normalization problem downstream with no canonical vocabulary.
options.condensedText: Two-week 25% pilot.

options.text: Add few-shot examples to your prompt demonstrating how to map unexpected property types to the closest existing enum value.
options.explain: Forcing 'tiny house' into 'house' drops real distinctions downstream â you're silently lossy.
options.condensedText: Verify 97% meets downstream requirements.

questions.pattern: Never deploy on aggregate metrics alone. Confirm segment-level accuracy before automating.

questions.text:
Your extraction system parses e-commerce product descriptions to extract specifications like dimensions, weight, and materials into JSON. Despite having a well-defined schema, the model inconsistently extracts the "materials" fieldâsometimes returning "cotton blend", other times "Cotton/Polyester mix", and occasionally omitting the field when material information is clearly present in the source.
What's the most effective way to improve extraction consistency?
questions.condensedText: 12% of extractions fail Pydantic validation ("expected float, got 2 to 3"). Retrying without modification yields the same failure. Best recovery?

options.text: Make the "materials" field required instead of optional in the schema to force the model to always extract a value
options.explain: Required-ness doesn't solve format inconsistency, and it can force invented values when material info is genuinely missing.
options.condensedText: Secondary larger-model pipeline.

options.text: Switch to a more capable model tier since inconsistent extraction indicates insufficient model capability
options.explain: Costly, and doesn't address the actual cause â the model doesn't know the canonical output format you want.
options.condensedText: Send a follow-up request including the validation error, asking the model to correct its output.

options.text: Set temperature to 0 to eliminate randomness and ensure deterministic outputs
options.explain: Determinism doesn't define the right format; it just makes the wrong-format answers consistent.
options.condensedText: Pre-process source documents.

options.text: Add few-shot examples showing 2-3 complete input-output pairs with standardized material description formats
options.explain: Correct. Few-shot examples demonstrate the exact canonical format you want ('cotton/polyester' as a normalized list), and they also raise recall on material info that was being skipped.
options.condensedText: Temperature 0.

questions.pattern: Validation failures: re-prompt with the specific error. The model can fix what it can see.

questions.text:
Documents arrive continuously throughout business hours and need structured data extracted. To reduce costs, you want to use the `Message Batches API` (50% discount, up-to-24-hour processing window). Your SLA specifies that extraction results must be available within 30 hours of document arrival with 99.9% reliability.
Which batching strategy is most appropriate?
questions.condensedText: Reviews ambiguous (brief) or sarcastic. Pros/cons are fabricated; sentiment is picked arbitrarily. Best schema modification?

options.text: Submit batches every 6 hours containing documents from that window
options.explain: Worst-case a document waits 6 hours to be batched and then up to 24 hours to process = 30 hours exactly. No safety margin for a 99.9% reliability target.
options.condensedText: Optional fields + "neutral"/"unclear".

options.text: Submit a single batch at end of day containing all documents from that day
options.explain: A doc arriving just after morning startup waits all day to be batched plus up to 24 hours to process â well over 30 hours.
options.condensedText: Confidence field + threshold filter.

options.text: Submit batches every 4 hours containing documents from that window
options.explain: Correct. Max 4-hour wait + up to 24-hour batch SLO = 28-hour worst case, leaving a 2-hour cushion under the 30-hour SLA to absorb batch variance and hit 99.9%.
options.condensedText: Allow null values for pros/cons; add "unclear" to the sentiment enum.

options.text: Use the real-time API for all documents instead of batch processing
options.explain: Meets SLA trivially but throws away the 50% cost saving the question asks you to capture.
options.condensedText: Empty arrays for pros/cons.

questions.pattern: Give the schema a way to represent “don’t know” and “can’t tell”. Otherwise the model invents.

questions.text:
After deployment, you find that 12% of extractions contain semantic errors that pass JSON schema validation (e.g., a duration like "30 minutes" incorrectly placed in an ingredient quantity field). Human reviewers have capacity to check only 20% of extractions.
Which approach most effectively allocates reviewer attention?
questions.condensedText: Missing source data model fabricates required values. Best schema design change?

options.text: Have the model output field-level confidence scores, then calibrate review thresholds using a labeled validation set.
options.explain: Correct. Field-level confidence lets you route the low-confidence 20% â which is where the 12% semantic errors concentrate â to humans. Calibration makes the threshold choice data-driven.
options.condensedText: Confidence field + threshold filter.

options.text: Randomly sample 20% of extractions for review, using corrections to track accuracy and identify error patterns.
options.explain: Great for measurement, poor for coverage â random sampling only catches 20% of the 12% errors (~2.4% total). Confidence-based routing finds far more.
options.condensedText: Prompt instructions to use placeholder text.

options.text: Prioritize review of all extractions where required fields are empty or explicitly marked as not found.
options.explain: Empty fields are a narrow error mode. The 12% problem is wrong values in populated fields, which this filter misses.
options.condensedText: Change fields that may not exist in source documents from required to optional, allowing the model to omit them.

options.text: Review all extractions from documents with formatting anomalies such as unusual layouts or mixed content types.
options.explain: A useful heuristic, but formatting anomaly doesn't reliably predict where fields got confused with each other.
options.condensedText: Semantic validation against source text.

questions.pattern: A schema that mandates fields the source may not contain forces hallucination. Make uncertain fields optional.

questions.text:
After your daily batch of 10,000 documents completes, 300 documents (3%) failed with "`context_length_exceeded`" errors. The results file identifies each failure by `custom_id`.
What's the most cost-effective approach to process these failures?
questions.condensedText: Valid JSON but 5% have empty arrays/null on required fields like citations and methodology, even when the source contains them in varied formats. Best fix?

options.text: Reprocess the entire batch with prompt caching enabled to reduce the cost of retrying requests with identical system prompts
options.explain: Re-running 10,000 successful documents to solve 300 failures is wasteful regardless of caching savings. And caching doesn't fix the length-exceeded error.
options.condensedText: Add few-shot examples demonstrating extractions from documents with varied structures -- showing how to identify citations in different formats and locate methodology details across section types.

options.text: Resubmit only the 300 failed documents after chunking them into smaller pieces, then combine the partial extractions
options.explain: Correct. Targeted, and it addresses the actual cause (input too long). Chunk the oversized docs, extract per chunk, then merge â minimum tokens, fixes the specific failure mode.
options.condensedText: Make citations/methodology optional + flag for review.

options.text: Resubmit the entire 10,000 document batch using a model tier with a larger context window
options.explain: Expensive (reprocessing 9,700 successes) and may still fail on truly outsized documents.
options.condensedText: Regex post-processor.

options.text: Increase the `max_tokens` parameter for the 300 failed documents and resubmit them in a new batch
options.explain: `max_tokens` controls output length, not input length. It doesn't solve `context_length_exceeded`, which is about the combined input-plus-output budget.
options.condensedText: Retry on empty required fields.

questions.pattern: When the model misses information present in varied formats, teach it with examples; do not bypass it with regex or human review.
